title: Saludos y presentaciones
description: Spanish greetings, basic introductions, and the inverted punctuation marks ¡ and ¿.

cards.notes: All-purpose greeting, any time of day, any formality. The silent **h** is the most common pronunciation trap for English speakers: *Hola* sounds like "OH-la", never "HOH-la".
Used until lunchtime (roughly 1-2pm in Spain). *Buenos* (m. pl.) because *días* is masculine plural.
Used from early afternoon until sundown. *Buenas* (f. pl.) because *tardes* is feminine.
Covers both "good evening" (greeting after dark) AND "good night" (when leaving for bed). Context disambiguates.
Standard farewell. The accent on **ó** is mandatory — *adios* without it is a spelling error.
Literally *until later*. Very common — more casual than *adiós*. Variants: *hasta mañana* (until tomorrow), *hasta pronto* (see you soon).
Always bookended with ¿ ... ?. The accent on **Cómo** marks the question word (vs *como* without the accent, which means *as* / *like*).
Standard reply. Add *¿y tú?* (and you?) to bounce the question back.
Reflexive: *me* is the reflexive pronoun, *llamo* is the conjugated verb. *Me llamo Ana* — I call myself Ana.
Literally *much pleasure*. Said after an introduction. The reply is *Igualmente* (likewise) or *el gusto es mío* (the pleasure is mine).
Uses *ser* — origin is permanent. Answer: *Soy de + [place]* (I am from + place).
Uses *soy* (1st person of *ser*). *Soy de España* — I'm from Spain. Note Spain uses *de* directly, no article.

steps.title: Greetings in Spanish
steps.body:
# Saludos

Spanish has time-of-day-specific greetings, like French, but the choice is less strict than in some other languages. **Hola** works any time. Add a time-specific greeting and you sound more polite or more formal.

- **Hola** — Hi (all-purpose, any time)
- **Buenos días** — Good morning (until lunch)
- **Buenas tardes** — Good afternoon
- **Buenas noches** — Good evening / Good night

A few cultural notes:
- Spain switches to *buenas tardes* after lunch (around 2pm), not after noon. Greeting someone with *buenos días* at 1pm is still correct.
- Latin America has regional variations: *¿Qué tal?* (informal hi/how) is very common in some countries, less in others.
- *Buenas noches* covers both arrival (good evening) and departure (good night). One word, two situations.

steps.title: ¡ and ¿ — inverted punctuation
steps.body:
## Spanish punctuation

Spanish puts an **inverted** exclamation or question mark at the START of an exclamation or question, in addition to the normal one at the end:

- ¡Hola! — Hi!
- ¿Cómo estás? — How are you?
- ¡Buenos días! — Good morning!
- ¿De dónde eres? — Where are you from?

The inverted marks let the reader know how to intone the sentence from the very first word. They're mandatory in formal writing; in casual chat (WhatsApp, text messages) many native speakers drop them.

On most keyboards: ¡ is **Alt + 1** (or AltGr + 1 on Windows international), ¿ is **Alt + ?**. Spanish-layout keyboards have dedicated keys.

steps.title: Match greetings to time of day

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish greeting with when you'd use it.
You arrive at a dinner at 8 PM. Which greeting do you use?
exercise.hint: 8 PM is squarely in evening territory.

steps.title: Introducing yourself
steps.body:
## A basic introduction

Three pieces cover most first meetings:

1. **Name**: *Me llamo + [name]* — My name is + [name] (lit. *I call myself*)
2. **Origin**: *Soy de + [place]* — I'm from + [place]
3. **Closing politeness**: *Mucho gusto* — Nice to meet you

Full example:

- *Hola, me llamo Ana. Soy de Madrid. Mucho gusto.*
- *Hi, my name is Ana. I'm from Madrid. Nice to meet you.*

The other person typically replies with *Igualmente* (likewise) or *el gusto es mío* (the pleasure is mine).

*Me llamo* is a reflexive verb — the *me* is *yourself*, the *llamo* is the conjugated *call*. At A1, treat it as a fixed phrase; the grammar shows up in detail later.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *Hello, my name is Ana, and I'm from Madrid.*
exercise.hint: Two clauses joined by *y* (and). Comma after the greeting.

steps.title: Asking how someone is
steps.body:
## ¿Cómo estás?

The most common follow-up to a greeting:

- ***¿Cómo estás?*** — How are you? (informal, one person)
- ***¿Cómo está usted?*** — How are you? (formal, one person)
- ***¿Cómo estáis?*** — How are you? (informal plural, Spain only)
- ***¿Cómo están?*** — How are you? (plural; also formal singular in Latin America)

Uses **estar** (to be), not **ser** — because how you're doing is temporary, not part of your identity. You'll learn the *ser* vs *estar* distinction in detail in Lesson 04.

Standard replies:

- *Bien, gracias.* — Fine, thanks.
- *Muy bien.* — Very well.
- *Más o menos.* — So-so.
- *Mal.* — Bad (be ready for a follow-up if you say this!)

Then bounce it back: *¿Y tú?* (informal) or *¿Y usted?* (formal).

exercise.prompt: Ask informally: *How are you?* (Include the inverted question mark.)
exercise.hint: Inverted ¿ at the start, accented ó in *Cómo*, regular ? at the end.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish reply to its English meaning.

steps.title: Saying goodbye
steps.body:
## Farewells

- **Adiós** — Goodbye (standard)
- **Hasta luego** — See you later (very common, casual)
- **Hasta mañana** — See you tomorrow
- **Hasta pronto** — See you soon
- **Chao** / **Chau** — Bye (informal, more common in Latin America; borrowed from Italian *ciao*)
- **Nos vemos** — See you (literally *we see ourselves*)

Spanish speakers often combine these: *Adiós, hasta luego.* is perfectly natural — it's not redundant the way "goodbye, see you later" feels in English.

exercise.prompt: You're leaving a meeting and you'll see this person tomorrow. Which is most natural?
exercise.hint: *Until tomorrow* — when you know you'll see them tomorrow.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to ask informally: *Hi! How are you?*
exercise.hint: Greeting wrapped in ¡ ... !, then question wrapped in ¿ ... ?.
